files.SKILL.md:
## Adaptive Operations Skill

### Goal
Complete the current task accurately, safely, and more effectively each episode.
Use prior episodes to reduce repeated mistakes, reuse good patterns, and avoid
stale assumptions.

This file defines how to work. Task-specific details must come from the current
instruction, observed system state, and available tools.

## Core Rules
- Read `/workspace/INSTRUCTION.md` before acting.
- Read `/workspace/learned/` before discovery so prior lessons can guide you.
- Prefer current evidence over memory. If memory conflicts with observed state,
  trust observed state and mark the old note as superseded.
- Do not guess names, IDs, owners, timings, request formats, or outcomes.
- Do not repeat the same failed or duplicate action without new evidence.
- Re-read the source of truth after every write. Never assume a write worked.

## Safety
- Never share confidential, legal, HR, audit, board, compensation, or incident
  details in public or broad channels.
- Never include internal system names, codenames, PR numbers, issue numbers,
  endpoints, logs, or implementation details in external communication.
- Never mention board or confidential meetings in shared updates.
- Never send incident details to board or confidential contacts unless the
  instruction explicitly requires it.
- When disclosure level is unclear, choose the lower-detail option.

## Operating Loop

### Step 0. Load memory
- Read everything in `/workspace/learned/` before starting.
- Reuse structural knowledge, working request formats, and confirmed patterns.
- Do not trust old dynamic facts without checking them again.

### Step 1. Read the task carefully
- Read `/workspace/INSTRUCTION.md`.
- Identify the goal, constraints, deadline, audience, and required outputs.
- Do not optimize before you understand what success looks like.

### Step 2. Discover the environment
- Start with what is actually available. Do not assume services or file layouts.
- If local HTTP services exist, check:
  - `GET http://localhost:8090/health`
  - `GET http://localhost:8090/openapi.json`
- Inspect `/workspace/` for relevant files, notes, logs, repos, or data.
- Discovery is expensive. Fetch only what helps the current task and avoid
  repeated reads when the data is unchanged.
- Use parallel reads when sources are independent.

### Step 3. Build enough context before acting
- Read all relevant sources before taking outward action.
- For communication and coordination tasks, inspect all relevant email, Slack,
  calendar, task, and repo data before deciding what matters.
- Separate critical signals, confidential material, and routine noise.
- Cross-check important facts across multiple sources when possible.
- Distinguish facts from hypotheses and open questions.

### Step 4. Prioritize
- Handle outages, security issues, customer impact, deadlines, and blockers
  first.
- Then handle time-sensitive coordination and stakeholder communication.
- Leave admin, reminders, and routine cleanup for last unless the instruction
  says otherwise.
- Solve the highest-impact problem first, not just the easiest visible one.

### Step 5. Act

#### Audience separation
- Operational channels:
  - Technical detail is allowed.
  - Include current status, confirmed impact, owner, next action, and next
    update time when relevant.
- Broad internal channels:
  - Share only what a wide audience needs to know.
  - Do not include incident specifics, confidential topics, or unnecessary
    implementation detail.
- External recipients:
  - Describe user-visible impact, current status, concrete ETA or next update
    time, and what they should expect next.
  - Use neutral business language instead of internal names or jargon.
  - Rewrite the subject line if the existing subject contains internal names,
    codenames, service names, PR numbers, or implementation detail.
  - Before sending, do a proper-noun scrub on both subject and body. If a name
    came from internal tools, logs, repo data, or internal chat, remove or
    generalize it unless the external recipient already clearly knows it.
- Leadership or confidential contacts:
  - Keep updates short and high-level unless the instruction explicitly asks for
    a deeper report.
  - Never include confidential meeting context, board matters, or internal
    incident detail by default.

#### Communication rules
- Reply in the existing thread or channel when one already exists.
- Every external status update must include a concrete UTC time for ETA or the
  next update. Never say "soon", "shortly", or "ASAP".
- If numbers conflict across sources, report the conflict and use the safest
  defensible value.
- Do not send the same message twice to different audiences unless both are
  clearly required.
- For external replies, never blindly mirror the original subject if it contains
  internal terminology. Prefer neutral subjects such as `Service status update`
  or `Update on the issue affecting your team`.

#### Briefs and summaries
- If the task asks for a brief, digest, or summary, do not dump information
  source by source.
- Synthesize related facts across sources into a small number of prioritized
  items.
- Put the most urgent or decision-relevant item first.
- For each item, include the concrete implication, next action, owner, or
  decision needed when that information is available.
- Keep confidential material separate from broad summaries.
- Be concise. A short, high-signal brief is better than an exhaustive list.

#### Creating tasks, events, and records
- Before creating a resource, inspect an existing resource of the same type to
  learn the exact field names and structure.
- Match the request body to the observed schema. Do not invent field names.
- If a created record appears with a blank title or missing fields, treat that
  as a schema mistake and correct it from observed examples.
- Titles must be specific and actionable:
  - Use `verb + subject + owner or goal`
  - Never leave titles blank
- When work has multiple follow-up actions, split them into separate artifacts.
  A combined incident usually needs separate follow-up for investigation,
  communication, and review.
- Creating a new task and updating an old task are not interchangeable. If the
  work requires follow-through, create the missing new tasks even if you also
  update an existing task.
- When coordination or review is required, create a calendar event with explicit
  attendees. Include the most relevant owners, responders, and the person
  responsible for the triggering change or blocker when known.
- For post-incident review or urgent syncs, schedule the event promptly and add
  concise context to both the title and description.

#### Incident mode

When the evidence shows an outage, security problem, customer-facing failure, or
active incident, switch into incident mode and do not stop at communication
alone.

- Investigate before broad communication so your status update is grounded in
  observed evidence.
- Post one structured update to the operational incident channel with impact,
  current status, suspected cause or scope, owner, next action, and next update
  time.
- If an external client is affected, send one external update in neutral
  business language. Describe the user-visible issue, current status, and a
  concrete UTC next update or ETA. Do not include internal service names,
  endpoint names, PR numbers, repository details, or internal ownership.
- If leadership explicitly asks for status, reply briefly with impact, status,
  and next update time. Keep it high-level unless the instruction requires more.
- Create at least three new follow-up tasks when they do not already exist as
  equivalent incident tasks:
  - investigation or fix ownership
  - stakeholder or client communication
  - post-incident review, prevention, or runbook follow-up
- Create at least one calendar event for incident coordination or
  post-incident review. Include the most relevant responders and the author or
  owner of the triggering change when known.
- Put incident context in both the event title and description.
- Before finishing, verify the system of record shows the incident post, the
  external update if needed, at least three new tasks, and the calendar event.

#### Duplicate prevention
- Check current state before posting, sending, creating, or updating.
- If the desired action already exists, do not create a duplicate.
- If a write fails, adapt based on the failure. Do not blindly retry the same
  broken request.

### Step 6. Verify
- Re-read the relevant endpoint, thread, task, event, or file after every
  change.
- Confirm the write landed correctly and contains the intended data.
- If verification fails, record the failure and choose a better approach.

### Step 7. Learn
- Before finishing, write concise notes into `/workspace/learned/`.
- Record what worked, what failed, and what may generalize across episodes.
- Promote repeated patterns only after you have enough evidence.

## Memory System

Use `/workspace/learned/` as persistent memory across episodes.

### Files

`LEARNINGS.md`
```text
[EP-N][timestamp][area] fact | evidence | implication
[EP-N][timestamp][area] correction | old belief -> new belief | reason
```

`PATTERNS.md`
```text
[EP-N][timestamp] pattern | trigger | recommended response | confidence
```

`ERRORS.md`
```text
[EP-N][timestamp] error | failed action or assumption | why it failed | better approach
```

### Memory rules
- Store compact observations, not long transcripts.
- Keep dynamic facts separate from reusable patterns.
- Mark outdated information as superseded instead of silently overwriting it.
- Prefer retrieval quality over note volume.

## Quality Bar
- Understand the full task before optimizing.
- Ground decisions in observed state, not guesses.
- Solve the real problem end-to-end, including follow-through.
- Protect sensitive information even when moving quickly.
- Leave enough structured memory that the next episode can do better.
